speaker: interviewer
text: About the song and the translation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Well, the translation is like this. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], what means as our comrades. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means when the Jewish blood from the, from the knife runs down the knife, then things will go twice as good for us in Germany. And hang the Juden. Hang the Jews. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was the communist. That was the song. And my father and I were standing on the second floor and just put the, the, the drapes a little on the side, trying to see as they marched by. And those were children, kids I went to school together with.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it, it just felt to me that it's, it's time to go. It's-- you know, you cannot-- we cannot stay there. There was no way. That, you know, that that was one of the first imprints on my life that I'd seen what they are going to do to us, that how we, we, we, we, we are just nobody. We are nothing-- dirt, like you would call. And at that-- OK. That's, that's what it was. And my father just in the beginning, he just waved it off a little. And but it, it came back. It, you know, it kept on going. It got bigger and bigger and bigger in, in all kinds of ways like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on, I want to go back a little bit and ask you how your family supported itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father besides we had a store-- we sold clothes. That means suits for men mostly. Or we also had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the, the material to make the clothes, to make, you know, where you needed a, a, a, a tailor to do this. So people used to come to the store. And my father fitted them. And if there was any thing, things to be refitted, or you know, the sleeve is a little too long, or the legs, or up here, or wherever, he had-- we had a, a tailor who was in the next town what was called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- how it was spelled? It was-- well, there was, there was a, a, a Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were about three or four sons. Their name was Kaufmann. And they were sitting like a tailor on top of the table with, with their legs across just like a, like a tailor of the old days and was sewing the suits together or fixen 'em-- fix them so they will fit the customer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was the store, your father's store, was that in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the-- tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Mhm. That, that belonged to the house. There was two big windows up front. And there, there were like dummies, you know, put on with suits and so on-- two big windows. But he did not depend people who come to him. He went out to get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a bicycle-- he had a bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the rise of Nazism, did your father continue to have customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, naturally, the customers might have just diminished. I mean, I was just a child there, too. But I know father went and came back a certain time, three, four o'clock. And many times, he brought home chickens. He had a-- there was a cage in the back of the car-- on the back of the, the bicycle. And he, he-- chickens or, or eggs what he trade-- how, I don't know-- for some other services he gave to people. And then we had-- he came home. My grandma took it or my mother. And we had cages down in the cellar. So we put the chickens in there. And what was very peculiar to understand that my grandma was very religious. So the chickens would have to be [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I don't know how they call that-- killed kosher why-- kosher way, the kosher way. And Hitler that time forbid it absolutely. Anybody who will do this, they'll-- will be punished or who knows, probably ship them into the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything was locked up, the doors downstairs. So we did that. My grandma-- you have to have ashes. There was ashes on the floor. And she took the chicken. Sometimes I held it, or my father held it, or my mother. And you, you pluck away the, the feathers. And then you cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you don't cut it through. You leave the head on. And then you hold the chicken up. And the blood will drop down in the ashes. That's is a way how we still killed the chickens. And my grandmother wouldn't have done it any other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your mother as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother, she came into this, into the, into the house in a way as I understood. My father, he, he married the, the, the, the, the father from-- married a woman who was also from his, from his mother-- not mother-in-law. But my mother had a, a, a brother who was married [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where, where, his, his-- my father's, my father's sister married the, the brother of my, of my mother. So it was a double marriage, so to speak of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she came, Grandma already was there. So she reigned the house. Grandma reigned the house. And my mother had to, somewhere on the line, listen to what Grandma had to say. Because she, she was the oldest one. And maybe sometimes, there was some friction in how to cook, and what to cook, what do we have? You know how that goes? Like, like a, she was the, the mother-in-law. She, she was the, the, the, the, he was the moth-- she was the, the, the mother-in-law. And Grandma was-- had, had reigned all over everything OK? She was the-- she was just a, the daughter-in-law-- not mother-in-law, daughter-in-law. All right. She was the daughter-in-law, so to speak of. And tell us what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother, in 1935, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had an accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 11 years old at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11. In 1935, I was 11. I was born in 1924. I went-- I was in school. My grandmother wasn't home. My father wasn't home, out on business. My mother was by herself. And we had a, a, a counter in the store. And it was in early spring, probably about March, April. It was still cool like it is down there. There was a little-- there was a stove, a coal stove, sitting in the, in the store. And my mother was leaning over the counter, reading the paper. Her skirt touched the oven where there was small, little plastic windows. One of them was somehow broke. And the, the, the coal-- you know, the heat and the coal showed right through. And there, she caught fire. I mean her, her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her skirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Skirt caught fire. And as she felt that, in her, in her-- how should I say? She, she, she was disoriented. And she didn't know or she forgot that she was all by herself in the home. She ran up upstairs, the next floor. And she called for my father. Or she called for somebody to help her. But as she was running, naturally, the flames got bigger and bigger. Finally, she seen there was no one home. She ran outside. And as she come running outside, the flames-- she was all in flames. Neighbors come running try to put her down. First, she, she resisted, as I was told. And but finally, later, they got her down to the floor and rolled her, trying to, to extinguish the flames. The neighborhood-- there was a little grocery store. And milk cans was sitting there. One of the neighbors-- her name, her name was Anna-- she opened up. And she took the milk and, and put it right over her in order to extinguish the flame.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't know anything about it till I, I was walking home from school. Kids come on to me and say, hey Munford, Munford. Your mother burned. I said, what? My mother burned? Yeah, your mother burned. Go home, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I walked in the house, people were standing around. I looked. There was a cellar. You know, you could-- when you walked in, down was a cellar. There was a, there was a little pile of ashes and parts of, of clothes. I said, oh, my god. She's dead. That's her. And I was scared. And then all of a sudden, somebody calls, calls out, Munford, come on. Your mother wants to see you. She's upstairs in her bedroom. So I went there. And here she was, laying, covered up till here. The hairs, everything was burned down. And she hold my hand. And she said, it's OK Munford. You, you, just you go down. You, you listen to, you know, what the people will tell you what, what they-- what you have to do. And that same day, they transported her to the hospital, the same hospital I was as-- in the kindergarten as a child. And there were nuns. They-- they did everything they could, I assume.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they-- after about three weeks, three, four weeks, she passed away. And I-- it was on a Sabbath, on a Shabbos. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I was in bed and I heard downstairs crying and, and, and screaming. You know what people do after they find somebody is dead? They, they-- you know, they, they disbelief and everything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, my father comes up. And he, he tells me, you know, your mother passed away. She's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I hugged him. I said, OK, Papa. I still have you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So from there on, the funeral wasn't-- it was in another couple days. And so we buried Mom. And that ended the, this episode. But I, I can remember weeks later, Father was always, you know straight out. And he-- I guess it come from World War I when he was in the war. And we were bicycling-- he with his and mine. We go to the temple to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], to the synagogue. So he said, you know, now your mother is nothing like-- left but just a skeleton. He looked at me. He said, yes, well, that's the nature of things. That's the way it happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I, well, I had to cope with it. But he, you know, he, he, he let me know that that's the way we all will end up. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after one year, we-- OK, we had to sit shiva, if you know what that is. That was for me the first time-- no shoes, only socks. And you had to sit lower than chairs, regular chairs. And every night, people used to come. You had to have 10 to make a minyan. And they used to come for a one wee, as long as the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] or the shiva was going. And they came. And I had to say the Kaddish. I had to learn the Kaddish. And they came, you know, for one week. And after that, we got back to normal life, as normal as it could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after one year, my father was looking for somebody to get married. Because I, the son, had to had somebody. I don't know, but he wanted to, you know, marriage again and not keep on, you know? And he-- I know from, from friends who are, who still alive who live in-- now who live in Florida-- his name is Ernst Kahn. And he told me, your father found someone.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's seeing her in Idar-Oberstein. It's another town about, I don't know, it took, took about maybe two, three train ride-- two, three hours train rides to go there. And he, my father beg-- begged him. Ernst, I want to go there. I know you have, you have a car. You have a truck. Drive me. I want to marry that woman. And she was good looking. And she was nice to me. And I never felt that she was a stepmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a good mother to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was. And it's just that she, she and her didn't come out, you know, from where. She did, she did a good job on me. And then my father was sometimes a little strict. She always used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Don't hit the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move forward now to 1938 and November and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and tell us what you experienced on that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. On that day it was early in the morning. It must have been about 5:00. Somebody knocked heavily on the front door. And I looked out the window. There was brown shirts. And they're called for my father to come out. OK. He come out. We have to take you in. We have to take you in protective custody, how they call that. So they took him in. They took him to the next jail, what was in [INAUDIBLE]. It was the next jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was up. My mother was up. And her sister also lived with us, with us. And I asked my mother, what do you want me to do? Well, Munford, what-- there is nothing you can do. Go to school. So I went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: By oh, by one o'clock, the school lets out. Take my bike. And I'm on the way, riding home. Here a colleague from the school who I knew comes. And one or two other schoolmates [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called me and said, Munford, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Don't go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why? What's, what's the matter? Don't go home. They're waiting for you. They'll try to beat you up. They'll try whatever, but don't go. OK. Go back. So I, I went back [INAUDIBLE]. And I know there was a, a, a, one Jewish woman had a, had a, a, a home there or was living there. I went to her. She was a widower, a widower from-- her husband passed away. Her husband was killed in World War I. So she was a veteran. How they call-- he was a veteran. But she was a, a, a widower and a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], they call this in German, a, a, a-- no, she, she-- they left her alone, OK, because of her husband who, who, who was killed on the German side for this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], like they say. And what happened with, with her? Did you stay with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I stayed with her till it got dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I started to go out, walking alongside the river. I had a pocket knife. I didn't know what I would have done with the pocket knife. But I took it. And I clicked it open. It was dark. It was November. And I walked along the river home, towards home. Excuse me. As I come home, I climbed up to the-- by the railroad and just peeked over. I wanted to see what's going on at home. The neighbor seen me-- Anna again. She said, Munford, come on. Everything is OK. You can go home now. And they took me by the arm. And they, they, they showed me to the house. And here's two brown shirts again standing there with the bayonets. And they see me and said, yeah, Munford. It's OK now. You can go home. Go inside. There wasn't, there wasn't a, a light in the house-- dark, pitch dark, glass all over the place. Nothing was, was kept intact. I don't know how we got through that night and waited till the next morning, the daylight. And later, I heard from the kids who, who tried to intimidate me and, and, and my mother, who was together with, with her sister at home when it happened, she, she didn't know what to do. She, she thought they're going to come after her or maybe kill her. She screamed, naturally, how women do in, in a situation like that. They, they always screamed, you know, in front of me. Yeah, your mother, ha ha, ha, in that way, you know, tried to, to make me look-- make her look how she was scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning, we-- first, we found out a few days later that my father was tooken to Dachau concentration camp, what was by [? Munchen-- ?] and with, I don't know, whoever, whoever was, was a man who was, was a, a Jew. We got-- we found out that we, we got a letter somehow. And actually, that scared everybody. And the way it was in those days, once you get into a concentration camp, the only way you come home is in a box or in, in a, in a, in a ur-- in a, in a what they call? In an urn.

speaker: interviewee
text: An urn-- and they get-- and, and the ashes in it. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back for just a second. That night that you were describing, what do they call that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Crystal Night, Kristallnacht. Why? Because everything was smashed-- the crystal, whatever they call it, the Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: The glass.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why it happened, I guess that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what-- and that's what happened to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to the synagogue in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That, too, the synagogue-- that was until later, I seen the synagogue, too. They smashed everything. They took the Torahs out. And they burned the Torahs, I think, things like that, all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you found out that your father-- when you got the letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Found out that your father had been sent to Dachau, then what happened with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was scary, very scary-- I mean, we all thought, no, now. What are we going to do?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- the only family we had who left Germany early was my, my father's brother and sister, who left 1935 who had a chance to get out of Germany. Because there was-- they were in a, in a district, [INAUDIBLE], where they had one year to try to get out, whatever they had, in order to, you know-- not to, to, to, to stay in Germany. So naturally, they went. They were the only ones. They were the ones we contacted right away-- everything by letter, naturally. And when they heard about that, they-- the only thing they could do, they had written a letter back that everything is done for Edward Wolf to leave the country. We have contacted the British authorities. And they have-- must have signed something. And then we send it to the commandant in, in, in Dachau. And it did help. And it took another week or so. And my father was left go and come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were-- naturally, we were happy to see him. And, and was a, was a great relief. He was ill. [INAUDIBLE] He had, he, he had a cold and things like that. He called the doctor. The doctor come and said, oh, he'll be OK. Give him a couple days and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after that, then we, we, we came into 1939. And my father told me now, you go to Cologne. In Cologne, you go-- we, we had contact with, with the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] they called it, or a, a, a kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], where I, I went there. And I had a little ticket on me. And they picked me up at the airport-- no, the, on the-- not the airport-- at the train station and brought me there. And there were young men and girls who we all lived together on two floors. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a place was this? Can you describe? It was in preparation for--

speaker: interviewee
text: In preparation-- kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was a preparation for Israel or Palestine. Those days, it was still Palestine. But also, it was a preparation to you should learn a trade. Or you'll be a carpenter. Or you'll be a, a, a mechanic-- not an auto mechanic, a, a a-- mech-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Machinist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Machinist-- there you go. I always mix that-- a machinist to know how to file metal, and to straighten it out metal, grind your, your tools, things like that, so that you have a trade. That's what my father wanted. And I was happy to do this, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're coming to the end of tape number two, interview with Fred Wolf.
